coredata.dc:title: Thermal wrinkling in anode production: Defect analysis and mitigation strategies
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd on behalf of The Society of Manufacturing Engineers.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd on behalf of The Society of Manufacturing Engineers.
coredata.dc:creator: Yun, Junyoung
Lee, Yoonjae
Kim, Minjae
Jo, Jeongdai
Lee, Changwoo
coredata.dc:description: With the increasing demand for electric vehicles, roll-to-roll manufacturing systems have emerged as a promising approach for the large-scale and cost-effective production method of Li-ion batteries. However, this process poses unique challenges, including web wrinkle defects, referring to undesired folds, creases, or distortions that occur in the flexible, thin substrate composed of copper film during anode production. Web wrinkles can occur because of strain deviation in the cross-machining direction due to the application of nonuniform tension and the differences in geometrical and material properties. This study analyzed the thermal effects on formation of web wrinkles with respect to operating conditions (coating thickness, web speed, and web tension) and suggested guidelines for battery anode production to ensure optimal operating conditions through a series of simulations followed by experimental verification. A simulation-based analysis was performed, and a copper film and anode material were utilized for experimental verification. Excessive wrinkling was observed in cases with low coating thickness, low operating speed and high operating tension. The results indicate the effect of thermal conditions on web wrinkling and the adjustment of operating conditions required to mitigate defects such as wrinkling.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Anode production
Process analysis
R2R manufacturing system
Thermal effects
Web wrinkling defects

Computational analysis of the drying section

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Composition of a Li-ion battery cell.
Tension application on the coated anode material.
Web wrinkle analysis model of the R2R dryer system.
Temperature distribution for coating thicknesses of (a) 43 μm, (b) 93 μm, and (c) 143 μm.
Visualization of simulated strain of web: (a) 43 μm , (b) 93 μm , and (c) 143 μm .
MD deformation for coating thicknesses of (a) 43 μm, (b) 93 μm, and (c) 143 μm.
Temperature distribution and MD deformation with varying (a) coating thickness: 43, 93, and 143 um, (b) operating speed: 3.0, 5.0, and 7.0 m/min, and (c) operating tension: 1.5, 2.5, and 3.5 kgf
(a) Drying section of roll-to-roll system, (b) unwinder section, (c) rewinder section, (d) slot-die coater, and (e) coated substrate at infeeder, outfeeder, and rewinder section
Wrinkle deformation in the side-uncoated area for (a) Case 3, (b) Case 14, and (c) Case 25.
MD deformation difference with (a) thickness deviation and (b) maximum wrinkle depth.
Geometry of the web and coated layer.
Material properties of the web and coated layer.
CFD model specifications and boundary conditions.
Quantification of wrinkle deformation and comparison with simulation results

head.ce:title: Thermal wrinkling in anode production: Defect analysis and mitigation strategies

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Mechanical Design and Production Engineering, Konkuk University, Seoul 05029, Republic of Korea

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Mechanical Design and Production Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Mechanical Design and Production Engineering, Konkuk University, Seoul 05029, Republic of Korea

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Flexible & Printed Electronics, Korea Institute of Machinery and Materials, Daejeon 34103, Republic of Korea

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Korea Institute of Machinery and Materials

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Flexible & Printed Electronics, Korea Institute of Machinery and Materials, Daejeon 34103, Republic of Korea

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Mechanical and Aerospace Engineering, Konkuk University, 120, Neungdong-ro, Gwangjin-gu, Seoul 05029, Republic of Korea

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Mechanical and Aerospace Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Mechanical and Aerospace Engineering, Konkuk University, 120, Neungdong-ro, Gwangjin-gu, Seoul 05029, Republic of Korea

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: With the increasing demand for electric vehicles, roll-to-roll manufacturing systems have emerged as a promising approach for the large-scale and cost-effective production method of Li-ion batteries. However, this process poses unique challenges, including web wrinkle defects, referring to undesired folds, creases, or distortions that occur in the flexible, thin substrate composed of copper film during anode production. Web wrinkles can occur because of strain deviation in the cross-machining direction due to the application of nonuniform tension and the differences in geometrical and material properties. This study analyzed the thermal effects on formation of web wrinkles with respect to operating conditions (coating thickness, web speed, and web tension) and suggested guidelines for battery anode production to ensure optimal operating conditions through a series of simulations followed by experimental verification. A simulation-based analysis was performed, and a copper film and anode material were utilized for experimental verification. Excessive wrinkling was observed in cases with low coating thickness, low operating speed and high operating tension. The results indicate the effect of thermal conditions on web wrinkling and the adjustment of operating conditions required to mitigate defects such as wrinkling.

ce:section.ce:para: The roll-to-roll (R2R) manufacturing system is a large-scale, high-efficiency production method suitable for flexible substrates, including plastic films, which are referred to as the web [1–7]. It has been used for producing solar cells, flexible displays, and electronic papers [8–14]. Recently, the rapid adoption of portable electronic devices and electric vehicles (EVs) has intensified demand for high-performance lithium-ion (Li-ion) batteries. [15–20]. To meet this increasing demand, R2R manufacturing systems have emerged as a promising approach for the large-scale and cost-effective production of Li-ion batteries [21–26].

However, the R2R process poses unique challenges, such as web wrinkle defects. These defects refer to the undesired folds, creases, or distortions occurring in flexible substrates composed of copper films during battery anode manufacturing [27–31]. The coated anode material undergoes a slitting process to yield battery-size pieces of the material. Following the notching process, the uncoated parts are cut off (except the tab area) and will be utilized as grounding components.

Fig. 1 shows the composition of the anode, separator, and cathode material for each Li-ion battery cell. With the defective tab material, the contact resistance increases, leading to voltage loss and decrease in output current of the product [32]. Furthermore, aggravation in the resistance near the tab increases the temperature of the battery [33]. Thus, the wrinkling defects in production of battery anode material must be reduced.

Web-wrinkle defects can be induced by non-uniform tension distribution caused by various factors, such as the lateral motion of the web. The lateral displacement of the web occurs when the rollers are not aligned in parallel (roller misalignment) in the roll-to-roll system, leading to strain deviation [34,35]. Even when other factors are ideal, strain deviation associated with the different thicknesses and material properties between the coated and uncoated areas in the cross-machining direction (CMD) persists. However, the thermal effects on strain deviation, which are a significant aspect of wrinkle formation, have not been fully addressed in previous studies. This study introduces a novel thermal analysis of the drying process, where the difference in thermal properties and geometry between the coated and uncoated substrate leads to uneven temperature distribution, thereby exacerbating strain deviation [36].

During the R2R production, the drying process is crucial for producing the active anode material. However, the difference in the thermal properties and geometry of the coated substrate results in an uneven temperature distribution, leading to strain deviation [37–40]. During the drying process in the R2R manufacturing system, the material is subjected to high temperatures and thermal gradients are established in the CMD of the coated web. Consequently, the coefficient of thermal expansion (CTE) of the web and its surrounding components differ, leading to larger dimensional variations and resulting in the non-uniform expansion or contraction of the material [41–47]. When these temperature-induced strain deviations exceed certain thresholds, localized areas of high strain deviation are created, resulting in web wrinkles.

This study proposes a series of experiments based on computational analysis, using a copper film coated with an active material, to comprehensively analyze the thermal effects on web wrinkling. We examine how changes in operating speed, drying temperature, and dryer irregularities influence strain deviation and wrinkle formation. Key process parameters, including web temperature and strain distribution, are monitored throughout the manufacturing process to investigate their correlation with the occurrence of web wrinkles. By focusing on the thermal mechanisms that contribute to web wrinkling, we aim to provide deeper insights into the role of temperature distribution in strain deviation and wrinkle formation during R2R production.

Web wrinkling in roll-to-roll R2R manufacturing systems, particularly in the production of lithium-ion battery anodes, poses a significant challenge. These wrinkles, caused by non-uniform tension, misaligned rollers, and thermal gradients during the drying process, result in unwanted folds and distortions in the copper film substrate. Existing methods primarily focus on optimizing process parameters such as web speed and tension, often overlooking the complex interplay between geometric and thermal factors. In contrast, this study takes into account both the geometric properties of the web and thermal effects, offering a more comprehensive understanding of web wrinkle formation.

The interaction between strain distribution, thermal gradients, and material properties makes it difficult to predict and control web wrinkling, compromising the quality of the final product and production efficiency. This study investigates the thermal effects on web wrinkling, offering insights into the combined impact of these factors. The goal is to develop optimized operational strategies that minimize web wrinkle formation, ultimately enhancing the quality, reliability, and efficiency of large-scale battery anode manufacturing.

R2R manufacturing is a versatile, high-throughput process used for fabricating products on flexible substrates. It involves continuously feeding a flexible web material through various stations for operations like coating, printing, and curing. This method is commonly applied in industries such as electronics, battery production, and photovoltaics due to its efficiency and scalability. The process can handle large volumes of material in a cost-effective manner, making it ideal for mass production.

In the R2R process, the web moves between rollers, and thermal treatment, particularly in the drying section, is a critical step to ensure proper curing of materials. Managing the temperature distribution and tension across the web is essential to maintaining uniform quality. While the process offers several advantages, the complexity of controlling these factors increases with thin substrates, requiring careful optimization to achieve desired results.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mathematical model of thermal strain of web
ce:section.ce:para: Lee et al. developed a mathematical model based on the law of conservation of mass that explains the tension behavior in the open-loop section by considering the thermal effects of a substrate under high temperature and tension [48,49]. The model expresses the thermal strain on the substrate resulting from temperature gradients and introduces the strain rate while considering the elastic modulus and CTE of the web. By considering these factors, the model provides a framework for understanding the formation of web wrinkles, as it links the thermal and mechanical stresses that influence the substrate's deformation.

The equivalent strain of the web within the span is expressed as follows:
(1)
ε eq

is the time-dependent tension and

, A , and
E eq
are the equivalent elastic strain, equivalent thermal strain, cross-sectional area of web, and equivalent elastic modulus, respectively. α is the thermal coefficient of the substrate and
θ 1
is the inlet temperature of the span.
(3)
E eq

ce:section.ce:section-title: Computational analysis of the drying section

ce:section.ce:para:
Tables 1 and 2
present the geometric and material properties of the web (copper) and the coated layer (copper and anode material), respectively. The material properties of both the web and the coated layer were experimentally determined at KOPTRI (Korea Polymer Testing & Research Institute, Ltd.) at temperatures of 20 °C, 50 °C, 80 °C, 110 °C, 140 °C, and 170 °C. Variations in mechanical properties, including Young's modulus, yield strength, and Poisson's ratio, influence strain distribution under uniform tension. The measured values were incorporated into the simulation, with intermediate values obtained through linear interpolation.

For copper, Young's modulus exhibited a decreasing trend with temperature, measured as 20.5, 19.8, 18.4, 16.2, 15.7, and 14.5 GPa. Similarly, the yield strength decreased from 198 to 170, 150, 130, 110, and 85 MPa. The coefficient of thermal expansion increased with temperature, measured as 0.5, 0.8, 1.0, 1.2, 1.3, and 1.39 μm/m·K.

For the coated layer, Young's modulus decreased from 117 to 113, 110, 108, 106, and 104 GPa. The yield strength followed a similar trend, with values of 220, 190, 160, 140, 120, and 100 MPa. Poisson's ratio increased from 0.16 to 0.21, 0.25, 0.28, 0.29, and 0.30. The thermal expansion coefficient also varied with temperature, measured as 0.47, 0.7, 0.9, 1.0, 1.05, and 1.09 μm/m·K.

Additionally, the thermal properties, including specific heat and thermal conductivity, exhibited significant variations between the coated and uncoated regions, further influencing the overall thermal response.

Fig. 2 shows the application of web tension on the coated substrate which is controlled by a driven roll, which is only in contact with the coated area of the substrate. Owing to the partial contact, the strain deviation in the CMD of the web is intensified.

The experimental setup and simulation study employed copper films with a thickness of 8 μm and a width of 150 mm. To investigate the influence of thermal effects on the process, the coating thickness was systematically varied between 43 μm and 143 μm, simulating different anode layer configurations [50–52]. The material properties of the copper film and coating layers were assumed to be isotropic, considering the fine-grain structure of rolled and annealed copper, which leads to near-uniform mechanical and thermal behavior. While real-world anisotropy effects may exist, this assumption provides a reasonable approximation for evaluating thermal and mechanical responses in thin-film applications Fig. 3 depicts the web wrinkle analysis model of the R2R drying section.

Table 3 outlines the CFD model specifications and boundary conditions applied in the simulations. The operating speed of the web was selected as a key variable, set at 3.0, 5.0, and 7.0 m/min, to evaluate its impact on thermal dynamics. Similarly, the coating thickness was varied to assess its role in heat transfer and thermal stress distribution. The operating tension was changed from 1.5 kgf to 3.5 kgf, while the drying temperature in the dryer section was maintained at 150 °C, with an ambient temperature of 22 °C. Convection coefficients in the dryer and room were set at 62 W/m2·K and 30 W/m2·K, respectively, to replicate realistic heat transfer conditions. These parameters were chosen to simulate actual manufacturing scenarios and analyze how variations in operating speed and coating thickness influence thermal effects and the formation of web wrinkles in the R2R manufacturing process.

The copper film and coating layers were assumed to be isotropic in the simulation model, meaning that their thermal conductivity, elastic modulus, and other material properties were considered uniform in all directions. This assumption is based on the fact that rolled and annealed copper films typically exhibit near-isotropic behavior due to their fine grain structure [53,54]. While real-world materials may exhibit some degree of anisotropy, prior studies have shown that the isotropic assumption provides a reasonable approximation for predicting thermal and mechanical responses in thin-film applications [55]. However, the potential influence of anisotropy on stress distribution and wrinkle formation should be explored in future studies.

To ensure the numerical accuracy of the simulation results, a convergence study was conducted. The energy equation residuals were set to a convergence threshold of 10−5, while the momentum and continuity equations were solved with a residual limit of 10−4. A mesh refinement analysis was performed to confirm grid independence, where simulations were conducted using progressively finer meshes until further refinement resulted in less than 2 % variation in key output parameters. Additionally, solver tolerances for displacement and temperature calculations were set at 10−3 to maintain numerical stability. The average number of iterations required for convergence ranged from 500 to 1200, depending on the complexity of the case.

Fig. 4 presents the CMD temperature profile of the web. As the coated substrate moved along the dryer, the temperature difference between the coated and uncoated areas gradually decreased. Owing to the wider convection region, the area with uncoated sides exhibited a higher temperature than the center-uncoated area.

Figs. 5 through 7
show the simulation results for a scenario with varying coating thicknesses (43, 93, and 143 μm), an operating tension of 2.5 kgf, and a web speed of 5 m/min. This case was selected to analyze the thermal and mechanical behavior of the web.

Fig. 5 presents the temperature distribution inside the dryer, while Figs. 6 and 7 illustrate the corresponding strain and MD deformation. Specifically, Fig. 6 displays the simulated strain along the MD direction over a web length of 0 to 5.5 m. To enhance clarity, Fig. 7 uses grey and yellow background colors to differentiate the coated and uncoated regions, respectively.

As the coated substrate moves through the dryer, the temperature difference between the coated and uncoated regions gradually decreases. The side-uncoated areas exhibit higher temperatures compared to the center-uncoated region due to their larger convection area. The temperature gradient along the machine direction was assessed at positions 0, 1.1, 2.2, 3.3, 4.4, and 5.5 m. As the coating thickness increases, the average temperatures in the coated, center-uncoated, and side-uncoated regions decline. Although the convection zone in the uncoated area remains unchanged, the reduced thermal conductivity of the coated layer restricts heat transfer, leading to elevated temperatures within the coated regions. Notably, the side-uncoated regions display higher temperatures than the center-uncoated region. The maximum temperature in the uncoated region decreases from 149 °C to 136 °C, as shown in Fig. 5.

Fig. 6 visualizes the simulated strain distribution along the machine direction (MD) for coating thicknesses of (a) 43 μm, (b) 93 μm, and (c) 143 μm. In all cases, the strain exhibits a gradual increase along the MD, reflecting the influence of thermal and mechanical effects as the web moves through the dryer. The difference in strain along the cross-machine direction (CMD) is most pronounced in the 43 μm case and least in the 143 μm case, indicating that a thicker coating contributes to a more uniform strain distribution across the web.

However, the variation is not substantial. The strain increase along the MD is slowest in the 143 μm case. This could be attributed to the reduced temperature gradient along the MD, which results from the lower thermal conductivity of the thicker coated layer. As the coating thickness increases, heat transfer to the web becomes more restricted, leading to a slower rise in temperature along the MD. Consequently, this slower thermal response may contribute to the reduced rate of strain accumulation, further highlighting the impact of coating thickness on the thermo-mechanical behavior of the web during processing.

Fig. 7 demonstrates that deformation in the uncoated areas diminishes with increasing coating thickness. The strain distribution within the uncoated region is influenced by the deformation characteristics of the coated region, which are primarily governed by the tension forces in the R2R system. Under constant operating tension, an increase in the cross-sectional area of the substrate leads to a reduction in total deformation, following the relation T = AEε . As the coating thickness increases from 43 μm to 143 μm, the strain difference between the center and side of the substrate ( Δε ) decreases from 1.04 × 10−4 mm and 6.0 × 10−4 mm to 0.28 × 10−4 mm and 1.67 × 10−4 mm, respectively.

In relation to temperature effects, the decreasing temperature gradient as the coating thickness increases suggests a corresponding reduction in differential thermal expansion between the coated and uncoated areas. The elevated temperatures in the uncoated regions cause greater expansion in these regions, exacerbating deformation differences and promoting localized strain concentrations. However, the thicker coating layer results in a more uniform temperature distribution, reducing the overall thermal expansion mismatch. Consequently, the deformation difference between the center and side of the web decreases. This reduction in strain disparity can help mitigate the formation of wrinkles, a common issue in web processing, by promoting a more uniform material response. As the coating thickness increases, the improved temperature regulation in the web leads to a reduction in web wrinkles, further enhancing the quality of the final product.

Fig. 8 summarizes the temperature distribution and MD deformation under varying coating thicknesses (Case 11:43 μm, Case 14: 93 μm, and Case 17: 143 μm), operating speeds (Case 5: 3 m/min, Case 13: 5 m/min, and Case 23: 7 m/min), and operating tension (Case 13: 1.5 kgf, Case 14: 2.5 kgf, and Case 15: 3.5 kgf). The results highlight the interplay between these parameters and their influence on the substrate's thermal and mechanical response.

Increasing the coating thickness significantly reduced the deformation in the side-uncoated area of the substrate. This is attributed to the increased cross-sectional area, which lowers the total deformation under constant operating tension ( T = AEε ). For instance, when the coating thickness was increased from 43 μm to 143 μm, the deformation difference ( ∆ ε ) in the side-uncoated area decreased from 6.00E-04 mm to 1.67E-04 mm. Additionally, the maximum temperature in the side-uncoated area decreased from 149.4 °C (43 μm) to 136.4 °C (143 μm), indicating improved thermal stability with thicker coatings.

The operating speed had a pronounced effect on the substrate temperature and deformation. As the speed increased from 3 m/min to 7 m/min, the time the web spent inside the dryer decreased, leading to a reduction in the average temperature of the uncoated section. The maximum temperature in the side-uncoated area decreased from 149.2 °C at 3 m/min to 137.3 °C at 7 m/min. Concurrently, the web deformation decreased due to the reduced thermal exposure, resulting in smaller variations in the elastic modulus. The deformation difference ( ∆ ε ) in the side-uncoated area decreased from 2.85E-04 mm at 3 m/min to 2.57E-04 mm at 7 m/min.

Additionally, with an increase in applied operating tension uniform in CMD, the web deformation linearly increased in accordance with Hooke's law (σ = Eε). The increase in deformation was greater on the side that was uncoated compared to where the center was uncoated owing to the large reduction in elastic modulus at various temperatures of the center and side-uncoated areas (140.0 and 144.0 °C, respectively). As the operating tension increased from 1.5 kgf to 3.5 kgf, the deformation difference (∆ε) increased from 0.354E-04 mm to 1.38E-04 mm and from 0.702E-04 mm to 4.14E-04 mm, respectively.

The results demonstrate that all coating thickness, operating speed, and operating tension play critical roles in determining the thermal and mechanical behavior of the substrate. Increasing the coating thickness reduces deformation and improves thermal stability, higher operating speeds lower the substrate temperature and decrease deformation due to reduced thermal exposure, and lowering operating tension dramatically decreased deformation difference. These findings are summarized in Fig. 6, which illustrates the temperature and deformation differences in the side-uncoated area for varying coating thicknesses and operating speeds.

Table 4 provides a comprehensive overview of the simulation results, showing the maximum side-uncoated area temperatures and MD deformation differences for all tested cases. Cases 3 and 25 represent the extremes, with Case 3 (3 m/min, 43 μm, 3.5 kgf) exhibiting the highest deformation and Case 25 (7 m/min, 143 μm, 1.5 kgf) showing the lowest. These results underscore the importance of optimizing coating thickness, operating speed, and tension to minimize web wrinkles and ensure uniform substrate behavior in R2R manufacturing processes.

Experiments were conducted to validate the simulation results. To represent a range of varying conditions, cases exhibiting minimum, median, and maximum deformation differences in both the center and side regions were selected (Cases 3, 14, and 25, respectively). Fig. 9 presents a schematic of the experimental setup, including (a) Drying section of roll-to-roll system, (b) unwinder section, (c) rewinder section, (d) slot-die coater, and (e) coated substrate at infeeder, outfeeder, and rewinder sections. The operating conditions for these three experimental cases are summarized in Table 5 .

Tension control in this system is achieved through a combination of feedback tension control combined with a dancer and open-loop draw control. The unwinding and rewinding spans measure the rotation angle of the dancer through a potentiometer, and feedback control is applied to regulate the speed of the driven roll.

Table 6 presents the common operating conditions utilized across all experimental cases. A copper film with a thickness of 8 μm and a width of 150 mm, comparable to the material used in the simulation analysis, was employed. The drying temperature was selected based on the required drying time for the coated anode material. The coating gap, which represents the distance between the web and the coater nip, was maintained at a precise 100 μm to ensure consistent coating application.

With increasing difference in deformation, the area where the side is uncoated forms a wavelet, forcing the uncoated substrate to fold and wrinkle on the surface of the roll. Various levels of wrinkle formation were observed. Fig. 10 illustrates the top view of the coated substrate for Cases 3, 14, and 25. Cases 3 and 14 showed excessive wrinkling on the side uncoated area, while the center uncoated area remained without any defect. Case 25 exhibited minimum defects with the lowest operating speed, lowest operating tension, and highest coating thickness.

To quantify the severity of wrinkling defects in each case, the average thickness, thickness deviation, and maximum wrinkle depth were measured using a contactless measurement system (SURFIEW, GLtech.CO., LTD). The thickness deviation was calculated as the standard deviation of the measured thickness data, with higher deviations indicating more pronounced wrinkling, suggesting a potential correlation between material nonuniformity and wrinkle formation. However, it remains unclear whether the thickness deviation contributes to wrinkle formation or results from it. The maximum wrinkle depth was determined by subtracting the average thickness from the peak value of the deepest wrinkle. The system used for quantifying wrinkle depth and thickness deviation is known for its high precision, with an inherent measurement resolution of ±1 μm. However, minor deviations may occur due to factors such as surface roughness or substrate misalignment, which were accounted for by detailing the system's calibration process.

To assess the reliability of the experimental results, multiple trials were conducted for each case (Cases 3, 14, and 25), with each experiment repeated three times under identical conditions. The results exhibited low variability, with standard deviations of less than 10 % for the measured wrinkle depths and thickness deviations, confirming the consistency of the data. The error margin for the maximum wrinkle depth was found to be within ±10 μm, based on the variability observed in multiple trials and the system's inherent precision, which is considered minimal relative to the overall wrinkle depth. These considerations provide a more robust framework for the analysis of wrinkle formation and deformation, enhancing the reliability and accuracy of the findings.

Fig. 11 presents the quantified wrinkle characteristics for (a) Case 3, (b) Case 14, and (c) Case 25. The average thickness of the uncoated area was measured as 4.76, 4.93, and 4.23 μm, respectively. The thickness deviation decreased from 15.06 μm to 11.84 μm across the cases, while the maximum wrinkle depth exhibited a similar trend, with values of 79.82, 56.83, and 31.29 μm for Cases 3, 14, and 25, respectively.

The formation of wrinkles can be attributed to localized strain accumulation, which arises from nonuniform thermal expansion during drying. As the coated and uncoated areas experience different thermal contraction rates, residual stress builds up, leading to out-of-plane deformations. The experimental and simulation results suggest that higher operating speeds and increased coating thickness improve thermal uniformity, thereby reducing localized stress gradients that contribute to wrinkle formation. Additionally, web tension plays a crucial role, as excessive tension amplifies the effects of thermal mismatch, leading to more pronounced wrinkling defects. In the following analysis, we quantify the relationship between these parameters and wrinkle severity.

Based on the analysis of the representative cases, the effects of key process parameters—coating thickness, operating speed, and web tension—on wrinkle formation were investigated. The following observations highlight how each parameter influences wrinkle severity and its potential impact on manufacturing optimization:
1)
Coating Thickness: The results suggest that increasing coating thickness helps mitigate wrinkles, likely by improving thermal distribution across the web. For example, Case 3 with a coating thickness of 43 μm showed a noticeable reduction in wrinkle depth and thickness deviation compared to Case 1 (with a 93 μm coating), where more pronounced wrinkles were observed. However, further increasing the coating thickness beyond 93 μm, as seen in Case 25 (with a 143 μm coating), led to a further improvement in wrinkle reduction. In fact, Case 25 demonstrated significantly fewer wrinkles than Case 3, indicating that a higher coating thickness can indeed provide additional benefits for wrinkle mitigation. Based on the observed data, a coating thickness of 143 μm appears to provide optimal results in reducing wrinkle severity.
2)
Operating Speed: Operating speed has a significant effect on wrinkle formation. Higher speeds improve thermal uniformity by reducing the time for thermal gradients to develop. For instance, Case 25 (with a web speed of 7 m/min) showed a lower wrinkle depth and thickness deviation compared to Case 19 (with a speed of 5 m/min). However, excessively high speeds can cause thermal instability and exacerbate wrinkle formation. In this study, 5 m/min or 7 m/min speeds provided improved wrinkle mitigation over 3 m/min, but optimizing the speed involves balancing it with other parameters like coating thickness and tension.
3)
Operating Tension: Operating tension is also crucial in wrinkle formation. When tension is set too high, thermal-induced stresses lead to greater deformation and wrinkles. For example, Case 25 (with 1.5 kgf web tension) exhibited lower wrinkle severity compared to Case 3 (with 3.5 kgf web tension). Lower tension helps reduce thermal-induced stresses, thus minimizing wrinkles. Therefore, it is recommended to maintain tension at 1.5 kgf for optimal wrinkle reduction, with higher tension values (such as 2.5 kgf and 3.5 kgf) potentially causing more pronounced wrinkles.

Table 7 presents a quantitative comparison between the simulation results and experimental measurements to validate the simulation model. The simulation results, represented by the MD deformation difference, show the predicted material deformation under various process conditions. These values are compared with the thickness deviation and maximum wrinkle depth measured experimentally. The thickness deviation was determined as the standard deviation of the measured thickness, with a higher value indicating more pronounced wrinkling, while the maximum wrinkle depth quantifies the severity of the wrinkles, calculated as the difference between the average thickness and the deepest wrinkle.

The comparison between these values demonstrates the alignment between the simulated and experimental results. In particular, Case 25, which exhibited the lowest MD deformation difference of 0.92 E-04 mm in the simulation, also showed the lowest thickness deviation (11.84 μm) and maximum wrinkle depth (31.29 μm) in the experimental results, indicating that the simulation effectively predicts the material's deformation behavior and its correlation with wrinkle formation. Conversely, Cases 3 and 14, which exhibited higher MD deformation in the simulation, corresponded to larger thickness deviations and wrinkle depths experimentally, suggesting a direct relationship between deformation and the severity of wrinkle defects.

Deeper wrinkles increase the tab material's contact resistance, leading to output voltage loss and temperature rise. As shown in Fig. 12 , the wrinkle formation, expressed in terms of the thickness deviation and maximum wrinkle depth, showed similar trend to that of the MD deformation results from the simulation. Case 25, with the highest coating thickness, highest operating speed, and lowest operating tension, showed lowest MD deformation difference at 0.92 E-04 mm followed by that with thickness deviation of 11.84 μm and maximum wrinkle depth of 2.57 μm.

By comparing Cases 3 and 25, the thickness deviation and maximum wrinkle depth decreased by 21.4 % and 60.8 %, respectively. The results suggest that wrinkle defect mitigation could be achieved by optimizing the process variables. In general, manufacturers prefer to set the operating speed to maximum for high production rate. From the results, the highest operating speed should be set by considering other factors, such as the drying time. Regarding the coating thickness, the current manufacturing of the anode material process includes a roll-pressing section that presses the coated material and increases the density. To achieve a higher density and lower wrinkle defects, increasing coating thickness is recommended.

The operating tension, typically set in the range of 10–25 % of the yield strength of the bare substrate, plays a significant role in wrinkle formation. In the drying section, nonuniform thermal effects can induce differential shrinkage between the coating and the substrate, contributing to strain accumulation and wrinkle formation. In this context, when tension is set too high, it can exacerbate these thermally-induced stresses, leading to larger deformation and more pronounced wrinkles. On the other hand, minimizing tension reduces the impact of these stresses, thus mitigating wrinkle formation. Our results suggest that careful adjustment of the operating tension is crucial, as it directly influences the degree of thermal-induced strain mismatch, which is the primary driver of wrinkles.

In addition to tension, process variables such as coating thickness and operating speed also influence wrinkle formation by affecting the thermal gradient across the web. Increasing the coating thickness allows for better heat distribution, reducing the localized temperature differences that typically lead to wrinkles. Higher operating speeds further improve thermal uniformity by reducing the time for differential heating, thereby reducing strain gradients and minimizing wrinkle formation. These findings emphasize the importance of optimizing process parameters—particularly coating thickness and tension—based on their thermal and mechanical contributions to wrinkle severity.

Furthermore, compared to the side uncoated area, the center uncoated area showed a significantly small deformation difference. As verified from experimental results, areas showing small deformation exhibited low level of wrinkle formation. Considering that the uncoated area of the anode material is cut off to form a tab region, multi-strip coating is recommended to harness a defect-less tab material.

This study provides valuable insights into the impact of thermal conditions on web wrinkle defects in the R2R manufacturing of lithium-ion battery anodes. By utilizing both simulations and experimental analysis, we investigated the influence of key process variables such as drying temperature, operating speed, and dryer irregularities on web deformation. The results highlighted the crucial role that uneven temperature distribution plays in generating strain deviations, especially in the uncoated areas of the substrate. Our findings underscore the importance of optimizing process parameters, particularly coating thickness and operating speed, to reduce deformation and minimize wrinkle defects.

The novelty of this work lies in its in-depth thermal analysis of web wrinkling, which provides a deeper understanding of how temperature variations contribute to mechanical deformation, a factor often overlooked in previous research. This research introduces a new perspective by linking thermal effects directly to strain deviations, offering a more comprehensive understanding of web behavior in R2R processes. Based on our findings, we suggest increasing coating thickness and operating speed while reducing operating tension as effective strategies for minimizing wrinkles during Li-ion anode production.

In terms of practical implications, these results can be leveraged to enhance the quality and efficiency of R2R manufacturing processes, not only for Li-ion batteries but also for other flexible electronic devices. By optimizing the process parameters identified in this study, manufacturers can achieve better product consistency, improved performance, and reduced production costs.

Looking ahead, future research should focus on the development and integration of real-time monitoring systems for active control of web tension and temperature. Such systems could allow for dynamic adjustments during production, leading to further improvements in process optimization. Additionally, incorporating real-time wrinkle detection and compensation mechanisms, such as spreader rolls, would help in minimizing defects and ensuring superior product quality. This direction will be crucial for the advancement of high-precision manufacturing in flexible electronics and energy storage technologies.

Junyoung Yun: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Software, Methodology, Data curation, Conceptualization. Yoonjae Lee: Validation, Methodology, Data curation. Minjae Kim: Software, Methodology, Investigation. Jeongdai Jo: Resources, Methodology, Formal analysis. Changwoo Lee: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Resources, Project administration, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the industrial Fundamental Technology Development Program (No. 20023578, “Development of digital twin-based dyeing equipment and process integration service platform using artificial intelligence”) funded by the Ministry of Trade, industry & Energy (MOTIE) of Korea and the industrial Fundamental Technology Development Program (No. RS-2024-00507660,”Development of autonomous operating system technology to improve productivity in synthetic leather manufacturing processes”) funded by the Ministry of Trade, Industry & Energy (MOTIE) of Korea.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Mechanical property anisotropy in ultra-thick copper electrodeposits

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Overman, N.R., Overman, C.T., Edwards, D.J. et al. Mechanical property anisotropy in ultra-thick copper electrodeposits. Appl. Phys. A 120, 1181–1187 (2015). doi:10.1007/s00339-015-9298-6

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Thermal stress analysis and characterization of themo-mechanical properties of thin films on an elastic substrate
Handbook of manufacturing engineering and technology

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Hu, Y.Y., Huang, W.M. (2015). Thermal Stress Analysis and Characterization of Themo-Mechanical Properties of Thin Films on an Elastic Substrate. In: Nee, A. (eds) Handbook of Manufacturing Engineering and Technology. Springer, London. doi:10.1007/978-1-4471-4670-4_51
